Methodology

Data Description

The dataset used in this study is derived from Mini-Challenge 1 of the 2025 VAST Challenge. It consists of a synthetic music knowledge graph representing entities and relationships within a fictional but realistic music ecosystem. The data is provided in CSV format and can be modeled as a directed multigraph for network analysis using Python libraries such as networkx.

The dataset is split into two files:

  • mc1_nodes.csv: stores the graph entities
  • mc1_edges.csv: stores the relationships between entities

Graph Overview

  • Number of nodes: 17,412
  • Number of edges: 37,857
  • Graph type: Directed multigraph
  • File format: CSV

This structure allows the analysis of artist careers, collaborations, production roles, and influence pathways across the music industry.

Node Types

The graph includes five distinct node types. Each node has an id attribute that uniquely identifies it in the graph.

1. Person

Represents individuals in the music industry, such as singers, composers, instrumentalists, lyricists, and producers.

Attributes:

  • id (integer): Unique node identifier
  • name (string): Name of the person
  • stage_name (string, optional): Stage name of the musician

2. MusicalGroup

Represents officially organized musical groups such as bands.

Attributes:

  • id (integer): Unique node identifier
  • name (string): Name of the group

3. RecordLabel

Represents organizations involved in recording, production, or distribution.

Attributes:

  • id (integer): Unique node identifier
  • name (string): Name of the record label

4. Song

Represents an individual musical work.

Attributes:

  • id (integer): Unique node identifier
  • name (string): Song title
  • genre (string): Genre label
  • release_date (numeric): Year of release
  • written_date (numeric, optional): Year the song was written
  • single (boolean): Whether the song was released as a single
  • notable (boolean): Whether the song is marked as notable
  • notoriety_date (numeric, optional): Year the song became notable

5. Album

Represents a collection of songs released together.

Attributes:

  • id (integer): Unique node identifier
  • name (string): Album title
  • genre (string): Genre label
  • release_date (numeric): Year of release
  • written_date (numeric, optional): Year associated with writing or creation
  • notable (boolean): Whether the album is marked as notable
  • notoriety_date (numeric, optional): Year the album became notable

Edge Types

Each edge describes a relationship between two nodes. The edge file contains the following attributes:

Attributes:

  • source (integer): Starting node ID
  • target (integer): Ending node ID
  • Edge Type (string): Type of relationship
  • key (integer): Distinguishes multiple edges between the same pair of nodes

Here, both source and target reference the id field in the node table.

The edge types can be grouped into the following categories:

1. Creative Roles

  • PerformerOf: A Person or MusicalGroup performed a Song or Album
  • ComposerOf: A Person composed a Song or Album
  • LyricistOf: A Person wrote lyrics for a Song or Album
  • ProducerOf: A Person or RecordLabel contributed to the production of a Song or Album

2. Industry Roles

  • RecordedBy: A Song or Album was recorded by a RecordLabel
  • DistributedBy: A Song or Album was distributed by a RecordLabel
  • MemberOf: A Person is a member of a MusicalGroup

3. Musical Relationships and Influences

  • InStyleOf: A work is stylistically influenced by another work or artist
  • InterpolatesFrom: A work incorporates melodic or musical material from another work
  • CoverOf: A work is a cover version of an existing work
  • DirectlySamples: A work directly samples another work
  • LyricalReferenceTo: A work references another work lyrically

Analytical Procedure

The analysis was carried out systematically to address the three core research questions.

Methodology for Question 1

1.1 Objective

Question 1 examines the internal structure of the Oceanus Folk genre and traces the specific career trajectory of Sailor Shift to determine her roots, collaborators, and indirect creative reach.

1.2 Data Used

The analysis used the music knowledge graph dataset:

  • mc1_nodes.csv for entity information

  • mc1_edges.csv for relationships

Relevant node types

  • Song

  • Album

  • Person

  • MusicalGroup

Relevant edge types To map the internal topology and legacy, the following relationship types were used:

  • PerformerOf

  • ComposerOf

  • CoverOf

  • LyricalReferenceTo

  • PartOf

1.3 Analytical Logic

To isolate Sailor Shift’s true referential footprint, the network was filtered using structural graph mathematics in Gephi.

Ego Network Anchoring The graph was anchored on Sailor Shift (Node ID = 17255). To capture all direct and indirect pathways without artificially distorting the community boundaries, the Ego Network Depth was set to MAX.

Intersection Filtering An Intersection Operator was applied to combine the Ego Network with a partition filter (genre = Oceanus Folk). This logic successfully stripped away professional networking noise and non-folk entities, isolating the interconnected core of Oceanus Folk music (Songs and Albums) that directly or indirectly ties to her catalog.

1.4 Visualization Used
  • A network graph highlighting the densely interconnected catalog of Oceanus Folk music, distinguishing between the collaborative core and standalone independent tracks.

Methodology for Question 2

2.1 Objective

Question 2 examines how the influence of Oceanus Folk spread across the wider music industry and how the genre itself evolved after the rise of Sailor Shift. The analysis focuses on three sub-questions:

  • whether Oceanus Folk’s influence spread gradually or intermittently

  • which genres and artists were most influenced by Oceanus Folk

  • how Oceanus Folk changed after Sailor Shift’s rise, and which genres became its main contemporary inspirations

2.2 Data Used

The analysis used the same music knowledge graph dataset:

  • mc1_nodes.csv for entity information

  • mc1_edges.csv for relationships between entities

Relevant node types

  • Song

  • Album

  • Person

  • MusicalGroup

Relevant node attributes

  • id

  • name

  • genre

  • release_date

  • written_date

  • notable

  • notoriety_date

  • type

Relevant edge types To capture musical influence, the following relationship types were used:

  • InStyleOf

  • InterpolatesFrom

  • CoverOf

  • DirectlySamples

  • LyricalReferenceTo

To connect works to artists, the following relationship type was also used:

  • PerformerOf
2.3 Data Preparation

Graph structure The edge table was used as the base table, and the node table was joined twice:

  • once to identify the source work

  • once to identify the target work

This allowed each influence edge to be interpreted using:

  • source work genre

  • target work genre

  • source work year

  • target work year

Time variable A unified work year field was created using:

  • release_date as the primary year

  • written_date as a fallback

  • notoriety_date as a second fallback where necessary

This derived year was used as the main temporal variable in all Question 2 visualizations.

Influence filter Only rows belonging to the five influence-related edge types were retained, so that the analysis focused on stylistic borrowing, referencing, and musical reuse rather than production or membership relationships.

2.4 Analytical Logic

The analysis depends on the direction of the influence edges.

Outward influence from Oceanus Folk To identify how Oceanus Folk spread to the wider music world, the following logic was used:

  • target genre = Oceanus Folk

  • source genre ≠ Oceanus Folk

This means:

  • the source work is a non-Oceanus Folk work

  • the target work is an Oceanus Folk work

  • therefore, the source work is drawing influence from Oceanus Folk

This outward subset was used for:

  • influence over time

  • top influenced genres

  • top influenced artists

Inward inspiration into Oceanus Folk To identify how Oceanus Folk changed over time, the reverse logic was used:

  • source genre = Oceanus Folk

  • target genre ≠ Oceanus Folk

This means:

  • the Oceanus Folk work is referencing or borrowing from another genre

  • therefore, the target genre represents a source of inspiration for Oceanus Folk

This inward subset was used to analyze how Oceanus Folk evolved before and after Sailor Shift’s rise.

2.5 Method for Question 2(a): Was the influence intermittent or gradual?

To evaluate whether Oceanus Folk’s influence spread intermittently or gradually, the outward influence subset was grouped by the source work year.

Measures used

  • Number of influence mentions: total count of influence edges per year

  • Number of unique influenced works: distinct count of source works per year

  • Cumulative unique influenced works: running total of distinct source works over time

Why these measures were used The distinct count of source works was used as the main metric because a single work could contain multiple influence links, which would overstate spread if only raw edge counts were used.

Visualization used

  • yearly line chart of unique influenced works

  • cumulative line chart of unique influenced works over time

These charts made it possible to determine whether Oceanus Folk spread through isolated spikes, steady growth, or a hybrid of both.

2.6 Method for Question 2(b): What genres and artists were most influenced by Oceanus Folk?

Genre analysis Using the outward influence subset, the source work genre was grouped and ranked.

Measures used for genres

  • distinct count of influenced works

  • raw count of influence mentions

  • comparison across genres

Why this was done The source genre indicates which genres were borrowing from or referencing Oceanus Folk. Ranking these genres reveals which parts of the music world absorbed Oceanus Folk most strongly.

Artist analysis To identify the artists most influenced by Oceanus Folk, influenced works were linked back to performers using PerformerOf.

Measures used for artists

  • distinct count of influenced works per artist

  • recent activity where relevant

  • notable works as supporting context

Filtering approach

  • only valid artist-work links were retained

  • artists were filtered to Person when focusing on solo artists

  • unique artist identifiers were used to avoid merging different artists with the same name

Visualization used

  • horizontal bar chart of top influenced genres

  • horizontal bar chart of top influenced artists

These views answered which genres and artists were most strongly shaped by Oceanus Folk.

2.7 Method for Question 2(c): How has Oceanus Folk changed with the rise of Sailor Shift?

To understand how Oceanus Folk evolved, the inward inspiration subset was split into two time periods using Sailor Shift’s breakthrough year.

Turning point used

  • Before 2028

  • 2028 and after

Why 2028 was used The project brief identifies 2028 as the year of Sailor Shift’s breakthrough, making it the most meaningful historical breakpoint for evaluating change in Oceanus Folk.

Measures used

  • distinct count of Oceanus Folk works influenced by each target genre

  • recent notable Oceanus Folk works where needed for supporting analysis

  • maximum active year for recency checks

Visualization used

  • side-by-side comparison of inspiration genres before and after 2028

  • post-2028 ranking chart of top contemporary inspiration genres

Why this approach was used This allowed the analysis to compare which genres shaped Oceanus Folk before Sailor’s rise, which genres became more important afterward, and whether Oceanus Folk became more hybrid and diverse in the contemporary period.

2.8 Validation Approach

To improve reliability, findings were cross-checked across multiple views:

  • time-based spread patterns were checked against genre rankings

  • top influenced genres were compared with top influenced artists

  • changes in inward inspiration were checked using both before/after comparison and post-2028 ranking

This reduced the risk of relying on a single chart or single metric.

2.9 Summary of Method

In summary, Question 2 was addressed using a graph-based, directional influence analysis built from the music knowledge graph. The method separated outward influence of Oceanus Folk on other genres and artists, and inward inspiration flowing back into Oceanus Folk. By combining temporal analysis, genre ranking, artist ranking, and pre/post-2028 comparison, the methodology was able to show both how Oceanus Folk spread across the musical world and how the genre itself evolved in the Sailor Shift era.

Methodology for Question 3

3.1 Objective

Question 3 aims to develop a profile of what it means to be a rising star in the music industry and then use that profile to predict the next Oceanus Folk stars over the next five years.

The analysis was divided into two parts:

  • Part A: Compare the careers of three artists to define the characteristics of a rising star.

  • Part B: Build a shortlist of recent Oceanus Folk artists and use the rising-star profile to make three predictions.

3.2 Part A: Build the rising-star profile

Artist selection Three artists were selected to represent different career trajectories:

  • Sailor Shift (17255) as the breakout benchmark

  • Szymon Pyć (551) as a long, steady-growth artist

  • Sienna Fox (17012) as a newer high-momentum artist

This combination was used to compare three different pathways to prominence: a rapid breakout trajectory, a long and steady accumulation trajectory, and a recent high-momentum emergence trajectory.

Part A1: Build the artist-career data source A separate artist-career data source was created to connect artists to their works.

Tables used

  • mc1_edges.csv as Performer Edge

  • mc1_nodes.csv as Artist Node

  • mc1_nodes.csv as Work Node

Join logic

  • Performer Edge.source = Artist Node.id

  • Performer Edge.target = Work Node.id

This data source was used to study career output over time, popularity growth over time, and Oceanus Folk candidate activity.

Part A2: Create calculated fields for artist-career analysis The following calculated fields were created:

  1. Is Performer Edge: Used to keep only performer relationships. [Edge Type] = "PerformerOf"

  2. Work ID: Used to identify distinct works performed by each artist. [Target]

  3. Work Year: Used to assign a year to each work. The year was taken from release_date first, then written_date, then notoriety_date.

  4. Valid Artist Work: Used to keep only valid artist-work rows.

  5. Notable Work ID: Used to identify works marked as notable. IF UPPER(TRIM([Notable (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)])) = "TRUE" THEN [Work ID] END

  6. Recent Work ID: Used to identify works in the recent period. IF [Work Year] >= 2035 THEN [Work ID] END

  7. Recent Notable Work ID: Used to identify notable works in the recent period.

  8. Artist Name: Used for display.

  9. Artist ID: Used to uniquely identify artists.

  10. Artist Key: Used to avoid merging artists with the same name. [Artist Name] + " (" + STR([Artist ID]) + ")"

Part A3: Build the three comparison visualizations

Visualization 1: Career Output Over Time This chart shows how the number of distinct works accumulated over time for the three selected artists.

  • Columns: Work Year

  • Rows: COUNTD(Work ID)

  • Color: Artist Name

  • Filter: Valid Artist Work = True

  • Artist filter: Sailor Shift, Szymon Pyć, Sienna Fox

  • Table calculation: Running Total This chart was used to compare the scale and pace of each artist’s output over time.

Visualization 2: Popularity Growth Over Time This chart shows how each artist accumulated notable works over time.

  • Columns: Work Year

  • Rows: COUNTD(Notable Work ID)

  • Color: Artist Name

  • Filter: Valid Artist Work = True

  • Artist filter: Sailor Shift, Szymon Pyć, Sienna Fox

  • Table calculation: Running Total

This chart was used as a proxy for popularity growth.

Visualization 3: Influence Growth Over Time This chart shows how often later works referenced each artist’s earlier work. For this visualization, a second data source was required.

Part A4: Build the artist-influence data source A separate artist-influence data source was built to measure when later works referenced each artist’s earlier work.

Tables used

  • mc1_edges.csv as Influence Edge

  • mc1_nodes.csv as Source Work

  • mc1_nodes.csv as Target Work

  • mc1_edges.csv as Performer Bridge

  • mc1_nodes.csv as Artist Node

Join logic

  • Influence Edge.source = Source Work.id

  • Influence Edge.target = Target Work.id

  • Influence Edge.target = Performer Bridge.target

  • Performer Bridge.source = Artist Node.id

This structure links the later referencing work to the earlier referenced work to the performer of that earlier work.

Part A5: Create calculated fields for artist-influence analysis

  1. Is Influence Edge: Used to keep only influence-type relationships.

  2. Is Performer Edge: Used to keep only performer relationships in the bridge table.

  3. Influence Year: Used to assign a year to the later referencing work.

  4. Valid Artist Influence: Used to keep only valid influence rows.

  5. Referencing Work ID: Used to count distinct later works that referenced the artist’s earlier work. [Source]

  6. Artist Key: Used to uniquely identify artists and avoid merging duplicate names.

Part A6: Build the influence chart Visualization 3: Influence Growth Over Time

  • Columns: Influence Year

  • Rows: COUNTD(Referencing Work ID)

  • Color: Artist Key

  • Filter: Valid Artist Influence = True

  • Artist filter: Sailor Shift, Szymon Pyć, Sienna Fox

  • Table calculation: Running Total

This chart was used as a proxy for influence growth over time.

3.3 Part B: Build the Oceanus Folk prediction shortlist

The three comparison charts were sufficient to define the profile of a rising star, but they were not sufficient to predict the next Oceanus Folk stars. A separate shortlist sheet was therefore created using only recent Oceanus Folk artists.

Part B1: Create calculated fields for Oceanus Folk candidate ranking Using the artist-career data source, the following fields were created:

  1. Oceanus Folk Work ID: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" THEN [Work ID] END

  2. Recent Oceanus Folk Work ID: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" AND [Work Year] >= 2030 THEN [Work ID] END

  3. Recent Notable Oceanus Folk Work ID: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" AND [Work Year] >= 2030 AND UPPER(TRIM([Notable (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)])) = "TRUE" THEN [Work ID] END

  4. Oceanus Folk Active Year: IF [Genre (Mc1 Nodes.Csv1)] = "Oceanus Folk" THEN [Work Year] END

Part B2: Build the shortlist table A shortlist sheet called Oceanus Folk Rising Star Candidates was created.

Filters applied

  • Valid Artist Work = True

  • Node Type = Person

Rows

  • Artist Key

Measures shown

  • MAX(Oceanus Folk Active Year)

  • COUNTD(Oceanus Folk Work ID)

  • COUNTD(Recent Oceanus Folk Work ID)

  • COUNTD(Recent Notable Oceanus Folk Work ID)

Sorting logic Candidates were ranked using the following priority:

  1. COUNTD(Recent Notable Oceanus Folk Work ID) descending

  2. COUNTD(Recent Oceanus Folk Work ID) descending

  3. MAX(Oceanus Folk Active Year) descending

This shortlist was then used to select the final three Oceanus Folk star predictions based on the “High-Momentum” profile.

Visualization Design

To support interpretation of the knowledge graph, multiple visualization types were used:

  • Network graphs to show collaboration and influence relationships
  • Timelines to show career progression and genre spread over time
  • Genre comparison charts to show how Oceanus Folk influenced other genres and how it evolved
  • Artist comparison views to compare rising-star trajectories
